1: And YHWH said to Moses, 'Speak to the priests, the sons of Aaron, and say to them' — the double address (YHWH to Moses → Moses to the priests) signals that the priestly legislation of ch 21 is mediated through Moses to Aaron's sons specifically; the priests are the subject of ch 21's regulations, not the entire people as in chs 19–20.
2: No one shall make himself unclean for the dead among his people, except for his nearest kin (le-she'ero ha-qarov elav): his mother, his father, his son, his daughter, his brother — the priest's general restriction from corpse-defilement has explicit exceptions for the seven closest relatives (vv.2-3); corpse impurity (Num 19) lasts seven days and disqualifies from sanctuary service. The priest's vocation requires limiting the normal human obligation to mourn the dead physically.
3: His virgin sister (achoto ha-vetulah ha-qerovah elav) who is near to him because she has had no husband; for her he may make himself unclean — the unmarried sister who has no husband to mourn her is included in the permissible exceptions; she remains under her brother's protection until marriage, and he retains the mourning obligation for her as for other nearest kin.
4: He shall not make himself unclean as a husband among his people and so profane himself — the Hebrew is difficult; ba'al be'ammav may mean 'as a leading man among his people' (i.e., he must not defile himself in ways inappropriate to his rank) or may refer to defilement for a wife's relatives beyond the immediate household. The priestly status creates a stricter standard than ordinary Israelites face.
5: They shall not make bald patches on their heads (lo yiqrechu qorchah be-roshecha), nor shave off the edges of their beards (pe'at zeqanam lo yegalleihu), nor make any cuts on their bodies — the same mourning-rite prohibitions of Lev 19:27-28 are repeated specifically for priests; what is forbidden to all Israelites (Lev 19:27) is especially forbidden to priests whose bodies serve in the sanctuary. The prohibitions align with 21:4: the priest's body must be maintained for sanctuary service.
6: They shall be holy to their God and not profane the name of their God (lo yechalelu shem Eloheihem), for they offer the LORD's food offerings (ishei YHWH), the bread of their God (lechem Eloheihem); therefore they shall be holy — the ground of priestly holiness: they handle the food of God. The expression lechem Eloheihem (bread of their God) uses the language of the divine table (Lev 3:11,16; Num 28:2) to explain why the priest's body and life must be consecrated above the ordinary Israelite level.
7: They shall not marry a prostitute or a woman who has been defiled (zonah vechalala lo yiqqachu), neither shall they marry a woman divorced from her husband, for the priest is holy to his God — the priest's marriage partner must share the holiness standard; a zonah (prostitute) or chalala (profaned woman, one born of a prohibited priestly union) or divorcee would introduce defilement into the priestly household. The prohibition is not judgment on the woman's person but recognition that the priestly household must maintain a higher purity standard.
8: You shall sanctify him therefore, for he offers the bread of your God. He shall be holy to you, for I, YHWH, who sanctify you, am holy (qadosh ani YHWH meqadishchem) — the congregation is addressed: they bear responsibility for maintaining the priest's holiness standards. The formula qadosh ani YHWH meqadishchem (I am YHWH who sanctifies you) grounds the priestly holiness requirement in the divine character, not merely in cultic regulation.
9: And the daughter of any priest, if she profanes herself by whoring (ki tachel liznot), she profanes her father; she shall be burned with fire — the priest's daughter who prostitutes herself receives the most severe execution (burning, cf. Lev 20:14) because her conduct profanes her father's sacred office. The priest's household represents an extension of his sacred character; its defilement defiles his priestly capacity.
10: The priest who is chief among his brothers (ha-kohen ha-gadol me-echav), on whose head the anointing oil has been poured and who has been consecrated to wear the garments, shall not let the hair of his head hang loose (lo yifra rosho) or tear his clothes — the high priest has stricter mourning restrictions than ordinary priests; he may not display any of the standard mourning gestures (loose hair, torn garments, cf. Lev 10:6) even for the death of nearest kin.
11: He shall not go in to any dead bodies nor make himself unclean, even for his father or for his mother — the high priest's prohibition extends even to parents; no exception is made as it is for ordinary priests (vv.2-3). The high priest's complete separation from corpse impurity reflects his unique role as the one who enters the most holy place; his continual cultic purity cannot be interrupted even for family bereavement.
12: He shall not go out of the sanctuary lest he profane the sanctuary of his God (ve-et-miqdash Elohav lo yechalel), for the consecration of the anointing oil of his God is upon him; I am YHWH — the high priest is confined to the sanctuary area even during mourning periods; his departure would profane the sanctuary by associating it with the corpse-defilement he would contract. The anointing oil that consecrates him creates an inseparable bond with the sanctuary that constrains his movements.
13: And he shall take a wife in her virginity (bevtuloteha yiqqach ishah) — the high priest must marry a virgin; his marriage partner requirement is the strictest of all (ordinary priests may not marry prostitutes or divorcees, v.7; high priests must specifically marry virgins). The requirement ensures the high priest's household is free of prior sexual history that could compromise ritual purity.
14: A widow, or a divorced woman, or a woman who has been defiled, or a prostitute, these he shall not marry. But he shall take as his wife a virgin from his own people (min-amav) — the four prohibited marriage partners for the high priest: widow (almanah), divorcee (gerushah), defiled woman (chalala), prostitute (zonah). The positive requirement adds min-amav (from his own people), which rabbinic tradition interprets as requiring an Israelite woman, not a proselyte.
15: That he may not profane his offspring among his people, for I am YHWH who sanctifies him — the purpose of the marriage restriction: to preserve the holiness of the high priestly lineage. Zar'o (his seed/offspring) must not be mixed with women whose status would compromise the priestly succession. The divine self-identification formula again grounds the requirement in YHWH's own sanctifying character.
16: And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying — a new revelation formula introduces the legislation on disqualifying physical conditions (vv.17-23), separating it as a distinct legal unit from the marriage and mourning regulations of vv.1-15.
17: Speak to Aaron, saying: None of your offspring throughout their generations who has a blemish (mum) may approach to offer the bread of his God — the legislation on physical disqualification is addressed to Aaron specifically (not to Aaron's sons as in v.1), because it concerns his lineage's priestly capacity. Mum (blemish, defect) is the same term used for sacrificial animals (Lev 1:3; 3:1; 22:20); the parallel between unblemished animal and unblemished priest is structural and deliberate.
18: For no one who has a blemish shall draw near: a man blind or lame (iver o pisseach), or one who has a mutilated face or a limb too long (charumm o sarua) — the first four disqualifying conditions: blindness, lameness, mutilated nose (or flat nose), disproportionate limb. These are physical conditions that would make the priest visually conspicuous at the altar; the rationale is aesthetic-representational, not moral.
19: Or a man who has an injured foot or an injured hand (shever regel o shever yad) — broken foot or hand (whether healed with deformity or actively broken); the term shever (fracture) suggests structural damage rather than temporary injury. These conditions would impair the physical actions of priestly service at the altar.
20: Or a hunchback or a dwarf (gibben o daqq), or a man with a defect in his sight (tevalul be-eyno) or an itching disease (garav) or scabs (yalefet) or crushed testicles (meroach ashekh) — six additional conditions extending the list: spinal deformity, stunted growth, eye disorder (cataract/spot), chronic skin conditions, and genital damage. The comprehensive list covers conditions visible, structural, dermal, and reproductive.
21: No man of the offspring of Aaron the priest who has a blemish shall come near to offer the LORD's food offerings; since he has a blemish, he shall not come near to offer the bread of his God — the summary prohibition: the blemish disqualifies from approach (yiqqrav) to the altar and from offering (lehaqriv) the food-offerings. The disability does not excommunicate the priest from the community or from his priestly identity, only from active altar ministry.
22: He may eat the bread of his God, both of the most holy and of the holy things (miqqodesh ha-qedashim umin-ha-qedashim yokhal) — the physically disqualified priest retains full access to priestly food portions: both the most-holy offerings (Lev 2:3,10; 6:17,25,29; 7:1,6) and the lesser-holy offerings. His altar service is suspended but his priestly identity and its material privileges remain fully intact.
23: But he shall not go through the veil (el-ha-parochet lo yavo) or approach the altar, because he has a blemish, that he may not profane my sanctuaries (mikdashay lo yechalel), for I am YHWH who sanctifies them — the two forbidden zones: the veil (access to the inner sanctuary/most holy place) and the altar. The priest with a blemish would profane these spaces not through moral failure but through structural incongruity with the symbolism of wholeness the sanctuary requires. The meqadishchem formula closes the section with the same divine sanctifying authority that grounds all priestly requirements.
24: So Moses spoke to Aaron and to his sons and to all the people of Israel — Moses addresses all three audiences: Aaron (head of the priestly order), his sons (the priests in service), and all Israel (who must honor the disqualification provisions). The broad audience confirms that the physical qualification system is public knowledge, not an internal priestly secret.
